text: The surgeon pulls the stomach back into its normal position below the diaphragm, reducing the size of the diaphragmatic opening. A mesh is placed over the incision to reinforce the closure. The surgeon performs a Toupee procedure, wrapping the top part of the stomach partially around the lower esophageal sphincter.

The surgeon dissects the stomach, esophagus, and hernia sac from surrounding chest and abdominal soft tissue structures, carefully identifying and preserving the vagal nerves. The esophagus is mobilized to ensure at least three centimeters of the distal portion lies within the abdomen, achieved through high mediastinal dissection.
The surgeon closes the enlarged diaphragmatic opening, or hiatus, using sutures to achieve a tension-free repair, ensuring the stomach remains below the diaphragm.

The surgeon performs a toupee fundoplication by wrapping the top part of the stomach partially around the esophagus. The stomach is moved back and forth to ensure it is not too tight, preventing restricted blood flow. The posterior aspect of the stomach is anchored to the diaphragm with sutures on either side of the esophagus, and anteriorly, horizontal mattress sutures are placed to complete the procedure.
